region_of_origin: China (Guizhou / Sichuan origin); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Fish-based soup set meal with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for sour soup fish set specifically; closest parent 'fish soup food product' (FOODON_03315595) used as ontology anchor.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (fish dishes) used as closest parent; no specific code for sour soup fish set meal exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.description: 1 set meal (fish soup portion with rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of sour fish soup (roughly 350 g including fish pieces and broth) and a portion of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from: (1) HPB Singapore Foodo — Fish soup (clear), per 100 g; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Cooked white rice, per 100 g; (3) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Fish (tilapia), cooked, per 100 g
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Sour Soup Fish Set' as a composite meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted blending of three approved-source components: (1) HPB Foodo fish soup (clear broth with fish) for the soup component (~64% of serving weight), and (2) USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice for the rice component (~36% of serving weight). The sour broth character (vinegar/tomato/pickled vegetables) contributes minor additional sodium and acidity. Values represent a plausible central estimate; actual restaurant preparation will vary. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice-based meals — analogous estimate based on: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sour Soup Fish Set. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the dominant glycemic driver being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice). The fish and sour broth components contribute negligible glycemic load and the protein/fat content of the fish component moderates the overall meal GI. A blended meal GI of approximately 54 is defensible per the mixed-meal GI estimation methodology described in Brand-Miller et al. (2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 74.25) / 100 ≈ 40.1 g (carbohydrate per serving = 13.5 g/100 g × 550 g = 74.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sour broth and pickled vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as the sour soup component can contribute approximately 1,705 mg sodium per full set serving, which approaches or exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the steamed white rice in this set meal contributes a moderate glycemic load (~40 g GL per serving); substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose response, while the fish and sour broth components are low-glycemic and protein-rich.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from broth; beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish component provides lean protein and beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids supportive of cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content of the sour broth warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are following a sodium-restricted diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sour Soup Fish Set (酸汤鱼套餐, Suān tāng yú tào cān) is a Chinese set meal consisting of a bowl of sour fish soup served alongside steamed white rice. The dish originates from the Guizhou and Sichuan culinary traditions of China, where sour broth soups made with fermented or vinegar-based bases, tomatoes, pickled vegetables, chilli, ginger, and garlic are a regional staple. In Singapore, this dish is served in Chinese rice kitchen and bento-style restaurants. The fish used is typically grass carp or tilapia, simmered in the tangy, mildly spicy broth until tender. The flavour profile is characterised by a pronounced sourness balanced with savoury umami depth from the fish and broth, mild heat from chilli, and aromatic warmth from ginger and garlic. The texture of the fish is soft and flaky, while the broth is thin and intensely flavoured. Nutritionally, the set meal provides moderate energy (~98 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from the fish (~7.2 g per 100 g), and relatively low fat (~1.8 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is sodium, as the broth and pickled vegetable components can contribute substantial sodium per serving (~310 mg per 100 g, approximately 1,705 mg per full set). The glycemic load is moderate, driven by the steamed white rice component. The dish is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. It is not suitable for individuals with fish allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sour Soup Fish Set as a composite restaurant meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted blending of HPB Singapore Foodo (fish soup component) and USDA SR Legacy (cooked white rice component), both approved primary sources. The estimation methodology is documented and defensible, but actual restaurant preparation will vary in fish quantity, broth concentration, and sodium content. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from HPB Foodo (fish soup, clear) and USDA SR Legacy (cooked white rice, tilapia cooked); no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo fish soup entry or the USDA SR Legacy rice entry used for this composite estimate. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source components used for this composite estimate. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo fish soup entry used as the primary soup component source. 5. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace for fish and rice components per USDA SR Legacy data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (54, low) based on the mixed-meal GI methodology with University of Sydney GI Database and Brand-Miller et al. (2008) as approved GI sources. 7. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant set meal portion; actual portion weights will vary by restaurant. 8. Sodium content is a primary clinical concern; the sour broth and pickled vegetable components are the dominant sodium contributors. 9. Halal status is assessed as likely true for standard preparation (fish, vegetables, vinegar-based broth) but individual restaurant certification should be verified. 10. FoodOn match type is closest_parent (FOODON_03315595, fish soup food product); no exact FoodOn term exists for this composite set meal. 11. FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent (A00LC, fish dishes); no specific FoodEx2 code exists for sour soup fish set meal.
